Automated development · design + reference

The Dev Loop

One work order in, one human-approved merge out. Every step is checked by scripts that verify receipts — never by asking an AI to police itself. Part one is the assembly line we designed; part two is the enforcement engine that already runs today.

Part one — the assembly line

Think of it as a renovation crew. You are the client; every job starts with your work order and ends with your key. In between, builders and inspectors are separate people, nobody gets to tell stories — only file forms with receipts — and a machine checks the receipts at every station. built 2026-07-02 · entry point /dev-loop

01

You open the job — /dev-loop

You hand over one work order: a Linear ticket, a GitHub issue, a spec file, or "next" for the top of the backlog. Nothing ever starts itself. The loop turns it into a task file, reads the repo's house rules (stack, test commands, conventions — via repo-preflight) so no builder ever stalls asking for them, and switches the enforcement on once for the whole run.

02

Survey the site — flow-mapper

A surveyor walks the existing code and maps every flow the job touches, including where things silently fail today. Look-only — it carries no tools, so there is nothing to enforce.

03

Draw the blueprint — architect

An architect writes the design spec for the change.

Receipt scanner appliesIts claims are checked mechanically like any worker's — evidence or bounce.
04

Argue the blueprint — team-review

Two reviewers with opposite instincts — a pragmatist and a perfectionist — debate the design directly with each other until they genuinely agree on one shared review.

New: consensus gate · gate_consensus.pyTheir agreement is itself a form a script checks: a real verdict value, at least 3 rounds of actual debate, and a file:line receipt on every accepted point. A lazy "looks fine" gets bounced back automatically.
05

Build and inspect — backend-dev / frontend-dev

Specialist builders are routed by which files the job touches; mixed jobs get both, in separate isolated copies of the repo with clear file ownership. Then the no-tools inspector (handout-verifier) walks in cold and re-checks everything. This is the enforcement station — part two below is its full anatomy.

Receipt scanner applies · 3 attempts maxClaims, banned patterns, scope, and the inspector's verdict — all script-checked, auto-retried.
06

QA

A QA agent exercises the finished work; failures loop back to the builder under the same script-counted retry caps — no self-counted honor system.

07

Argue the result — team-review again

The same two reviewers now debate the finished change. If their joint verdict is "fix first," the accepted findings — each carrying a file:line receipt — automatically become a fresh work order back at step 05. Two rework cycles maximum, then the loop stops and escalates to you.

08

Delivery — your eyes, your key

You get exactly three lines (pre-merge-review): where to see it running, the one thing only a human eye can judge, and what it does in plain English. Deliberately human, not mechanized. The merge itself stays locked until /approve-merge mints your one-time key — consumed on use.

What happens to each existing piece

PieceFateWhy
handout gates + hookskept · live todayBecomes the shared receipt scanner for every station, not just its own.
handout-verifierkeptThe no-tools inspector, unchanged.
flow-mapperkept · new roleSite surveyor at step 02.
backend-dev / frontend-devkeptThe builders, now wearing the receipt contract in their instructions.
team-reviewelevatedRuns twice (blueprint + result); its consensus is now machine-checked.
pre-merge-review + merge lockkept · untouchedThe human key. Deliberately not mechanized.
feature-dev-team internal review loopsretiredHonor-system self-review, replaced by the scanner + the debates. Keeps only job-sizing: tiny changes skip the whole line.
handout-worker (generic builder)retiredAny specialist can wear the form-and-receipts contract; no need for a generic one.

What was built (2026-07-02)

Five pieces, all live: the consensus-checking script (gate_consensus.py, wired into the SubagentStop hook alongside a new rule that turns ending in a teammate message are mid-debate and never gated), the /dev-loop entry skill with the rework-cycle counter, the builder contract template (dev-loop/contract.md), the team-review consensus-block requirement, and the feature-dev-team demotion. The consensus gate was verified against seven fabricated transcripts (valid consensus passes; too-few exchanges, fake round counts, dead evidence, evidence-free fix-first, and invalid verdicts all bounce) plus an end-to-end hook test of the armed retry path.

Part two — inside the enforcement engine: /handout

This is the receipt scanner every station above relies on. It exists and runs today; everything below is documentation of working code, not design.

Why it counts as mechanical

Every enforcement point follows the same three-part pattern. The agent's cooperation is only needed to emit the block — and if it doesn't, that itself is a parse failure that sends it back.

01

Structured contract

The agent must end its turn with a fenced ql-result or ql-verdict block with typed fields — status enums, evidence refs, file:line pointers.

02

Script parses, not a model

A hook fires on SubagentStop/Stop and runs real Python against the block, the transcript JSONL, and the actual git diff.

03

Exit code enforces

Failure prints findings to stderr and exits 2 — Claude Code sends the same agent back to fix it. Three strikes and the hook gives up so the lead can escalate.

Lifecycle of one armed task

00

Arming — deterministic, not model-dependent

The skill's frontmatter pre-authorizes Bash(touch *) / Bash(mkdir *), so a literal ! shell line runs the instant the skill loads: it creates the marker state/active-<sha1(cwd)[:16]>. Every hook checks this marker first — no marker (or one older than 180 min), no gating. Teeth are opt-in per directory because an always-on Stop gate previously over-litigated normal conversation.

01

Worker builds — handout-worker

An isolated builder agent (has Edit/Write/Bash) executes the task and must end with one ql-result block: a status, claims, and for each claim a piece of evidence it produced this turn.

SubagentStop · gate-subagent-stop.sh · exit 2 on failgate_claims + gate_bans + gate_altitude (hard) · gate_scope (warn) — up to 3 attempts per agent
02

Verifier attacks — handout-verifier

A fresh-context adversary that never saw the builder's reasoning re-runs every claim itself and hunts for the hole. It has no Edit/Write — it judges, it never fixes. It ends with one ql-verdict block.

SubagentStop · same hook, detects the verdict blockgate_verdict — a pass needs evidence that actually ran (no rubber-stamping); a fail needs a concrete issue + where (no vague objections)
03

Lead reports

The main session summarizes the outcome at the right altitude. Its own final message is gated too.

Stop · gate-stop.sh · JSON block decision on failgate_bans + gate_altitude with --role lead — a question gets ≤14 lines of prose, no code blocks, no tables
04

Merge stays locked

Always-on and global (not gated by the marker): any git merge, force-push, push to main, or gh pr merge is denied unless a one-shot approval token exists.

PreToolUse (Bash) · merge-guard.sh · permission denymerge_match.py parses compound commands; /approve-merge mints state/merge-approved, consumed on first use
05

Disarm

On completion the marker is removed and the decision ledger cleared; otherwise the marker self-expires after 180 minutes so a stale task can't gate tomorrow's session.

The two contract blocks

These are the only interface between agents and gates. The gates read the block — never the surrounding prose.

ql-result — worker output

status: completed | working | input-required | failed
summary: one line
claims:
  - claim: tests pass
    evidence:
      type: command | file | url
      ref: npm test
      result: 42 passed
files_changed:
  - src/auth/session.ts
blocking_question: (if input-required)

ql-verdict — verifier output

verdict: pass | fail
summary: one line
checks:
  - check: re-ran the test suite
    evidence:
      type: command
      ref: npm test
      result: 42 passed
findings: (required on fail)
  - severity: blocking | minor
    issue: what is wrong
    where: path/to/file.ts:88

Gate reference

gate_claims.py hard · worker

The anti-"trust me, it's done" gate. Parses ql-result and cross-examines it against reality:

  • Evidence must have happened. A command ref must match a Bash command that actually ran this turn; a file ref must be path:line where the file exists and the line number is real; a url must be well-formed.
  • The diff can't be hidden. files_changed must cover every file git says changed — an unlisted change is a failure.
  • Status semantics are enforced. completed requires a non-empty diff and evidenced claims; input-required requires a non-empty blocking_question; working passes clean without retry.

gate_verdict.py hard · verifier

Keeps the verifier honest in both directions. Anti-rubber-stamp: at least one check must carry evidence that verifiably ran, so "looks good to me" can't pass. Anti-vague-fail: a fail verdict needs at least one finding with both a concrete issue and a where (path:line), so lazy objections can't block.

gate_bans.py hard · worker + lead

Regex rules over added diff lines only, from config/bans.txt (kind::regex::message). Real examples: narrating comments (// set the value…), type/interface declarations inside .service.ts/.controller.ts files, and concept bans — decisions already made that must not creep back in ("subjectId was explicitly dropped", "SerpApi was never requested"). Findings deduped, capped at 25.

gate_altitude.py hard · worker + lead

Fires only when the last user message was a question. Then the answer must be a thesis, not a wall: ≤14 non-blank lines, no code blocks, no file:line refs, fewer than two headers, no tables — and a question turn must not have used Edit/Write (answering a question is not a license to change code).

gate_scope.py soft · warn only

The over-engineering tripwire — warns, never blocks. Triggers on: a small-ask ("just…", "simple", "one-liner", under 240 chars) whose diff touches more than 4 files; infra sneaking into the diff (new package versions, docker, new Redis, queues, helm, k8s Deployments, Terraform resources); more than 8 agents spawned; more than 5 background shells.

verify.py orchestrator

Maps role to gate set — worker → claims + bans + altitude, lead → bans + altitude, verifier → verdict; scope always runs soft. Emits {"pass": bool, "blocks": […], "warnings": […]} for the hooks to act on.

merge_match.py · decision_ledger.py · qllib.py support

  • merge_match splits compound commands (&&, ;, pipes), strips env/sudo prefixes, and flags git merge, force-pushes, pushes targeting main/master, and gh pr merge — so a merge can't hide inside a chained command.
  • decision_ledger keeps an append-only "SETTLED; do not reopen" list per directory; inject-ledger.sh re-injects it on every prompt while armed, so settled decisions survive context loss.
  • qllib is the shared parser: reads the transcript JSONL, distinguishes real user messages from system noise, extracts this turn's tool calls and Bash commands, and computes the live git diff — the ground truth every gate checks against.

Hook wiring

Hook eventScriptActs whenOn failure
SubagentStopgate-subagent-stop.shMarker present & freshexit 2 — agent sent back with findings, max 3 attempts (counter in state/attempts-<agent>)
Stopgate-stop.shMarker present & freshJSON {"decision":"block"} — lead's reply rejected with reason
PreToolUse (Bash)merge-guard.shAlways (global)permissionDecision: deny unless one-shot token exists
UserPromptSubmitinject-ledger.shMarker presentn/a — injects the decision ledger as context

The numbers

3retry attempts per agent before the hook gives up
180 minmarker TTL — a stale task self-disarms
14max non-blank lines answering a question
4files touched before a "small ask" warns
8 / 5max agents / background shells before scope warns
1merge per /approve-merge — token consumed on use

The two agents under the gates

handout-worker builds

Executes one task end-to-end in its own context: ground the task in one sentence, build the smallest correct change, prove every claim by actually running the check this turn. Never merges.

Has: Read, Edit, Write, MultiEdit, Bash, Grep, Glob, WebFetch/Search

handout-verifier judges

Fresh context, never saw the builder's reasoning. Distrusts every claim and re-runs it independently; must either name a concrete path:line defect or pass.

Has: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, WebFetch/Search · Lacks: Edit, Write, MultiEdit — it can never "fix it real quick"

The point

Trust nothing that can't exit 2.

Prompt rules degrade under context pressure; a hook does not. The worker can't claim "done" without a diff and live evidence, the verifier can't rubber-stamp or vaguely object, the lead can't answer a question with a wall of code, and nothing merges without a human minting a one-shot token. The only trust left in the loop is the one that belongs there: yours, at /approve-merge.

Enforcement engine: ~/.claude/skills/handout/ (gates, hooks, agents, config/bans.txt) + hook entries in ~/.claude/settings.json · built 2026-06-26 from a 4-month friction audit · assembly line built 2026-07-02 · entry: /dev-loop
